title: Animal vs plant cells — what's different?
examTip: AQA loves asking you to compare animal and plant cells, or explain why a specialised cell looks the way it does. Know the three plant-only structures, and be ready to link every cell feature to its function.
introTips: This lesson covers the extra structures that plant cells have — and why they need them.
You'll also look at specialised cells: both animal and plant versions, and exactly which features each one has and why.
The differentiation idea ties everything together — cells don't start specialised, they become specialised.
intro: Every cell you've seen so far has been the basic version. But your body has over 200 different cell types — each one shaped and equipped for a specific job. Plants do the same thing. This lesson is about what makes cells different from each other, and why.

keyPoints.heading: The three extras plant cells have
keyPoints.content: Plant cells have everything animal cells have — nucleus, cytoplasm, cell membrane, mitochondria, ribosomes — plus three more. A cell wall made of cellulose gives the cell rigid support (animal cells have no wall). Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll, which captures light energy for photosynthesis (animal cells can't photosynthesise). A permanent vacuole filled with cell sap keeps the cell firm and maintains its shape. These three structures tell you immediately that you're looking at a plant cell.
writeBullets: Cell wall — made of cellulose; gives rigid support and shape
Chloroplasts — contain chlorophyll; absorb light for photosynthesis
Permanent vacuole — filled with cell sap; keeps the cell turgid (firm)
Animal cells have NONE of these three structures
Both cell types share: nucleus, cytoplasm, cell membrane, mitochondria, ribosomes

keyTerms.def: Rigid layer outside the cell membrane in plant cells, made of cellulose — provides structural support
Organelle in plant cells that contains chlorophyll and carries out photosynthesis
A membrane-bound space in plant cells filled with cell sap; keeps the cell turgid
A tough carbohydrate polymer that makes up plant cell walls
The green pigment inside chloroplasts that absorbs light energy for photosynthesis

keyPoints.memTip: Three plant extras: CCV — Cell wall, Chloroplasts, Vacuole. Say it three times: CCV. If a question asks what plant cells have that animal cells don't, those three letters are your answer. Then add detail for each one.

keyPoints.heading: Specialised animal cells
keyPoints.content: Sperm cell: built to deliver DNA to an egg. Has an acrosome (enzyme-filled cap that digests through the egg's outer layer), packed mitochondria in the middle piece (for the energy needed to swim), and a tail (flagellum) to propel it. Egg cell (ovum): built to be fertilised and then support early development. Has a huge amount of cytoplasm storing nutrients, and a zona pellucida (jelly coat) that hardens after one sperm enters — preventing polyspermy. Red blood cell: built to carry oxygen efficiently. Biconcave disc shape increases surface area. No nucleus — leaves more room for haemoglobin. Flexible enough to squeeze through capillaries. Nerve cell (neurone): built to carry electrical signals over long distances. Has a long axon, a myelin sheath (insulation that speeds up signals), and synaptic knobs at the end to pass signals to the next cell.
writeBullets: Egg: large cytoplasm (nutrients), zona pellucida (blocks extra sperm)
Red blood cell: biconcave shape (↑ surface area), no nucleus (more space for haemoglobin), haemoglobin (carries O₂)
Nerve cell: long axon (carries signal far), myelin sheath (insulation, speeds signal), synaptic knobs (pass signal on)

keyTerms.def: Enzyme-containing cap on a sperm cell that digests the outer layer of the egg
The jelly-like coat around an egg cell; hardens after fertilisation to prevent more sperm entering
A disc shape that is concave (curved inward) on both sides — increases surface area relative to volume
The red, iron-containing protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen
The long extension of a nerve cell that carries electrical impulses away from the cell body
A fatty insulating layer around the axon of a nerve cell that speeds up electrical signals
The terminal end of a nerve cell axon that releases neurotransmitters to pass a signal to the next cell

keyPoints.memTip: For each specialised cell, practise linking structure to function. Sperm = swimmer (tail + energy). Egg = survivor (food store + protection). RBC = carrier (shape + haemoglobin). Nerve = communicator (length + insulation). If you can explain WHY each feature exists, you'll always score the full marks.

keyPoints.heading: Specialised plant cells
keyPoints.content: Root hair cell: its job is to absorb water and minerals from the soil. The long hair-like extension creates a huge surface area to maximise absorption. Root hair cells are underground, so they have no chloroplasts — photosynthesis doesn't happen here. Palisade mesophyll cell: its job is to photosynthesise. It is packed with chloroplasts to capture as much light as possible. These cells sit at the top of the leaf, closest to the light source — and they're tall and tightly packed so light passes through as many chloroplasts as possible before it gets through.
writeBullets: Root hair cell: large surface area (long hair extension), no chloroplasts (underground — no light)
Palisade mesophyll: packed with chloroplasts, positioned at top of leaf to maximise light absorption
Both examples: structure matches function exactly

keyTerms.def: A specialised plant cell with a long hair-like extension that increases surface area for absorbing water and minerals
A tall, chloroplast-packed plant cell near the top of a leaf that carries out most of the photosynthesis

keyPoints.memTip: Root hair = no light, needs water → no chloroplasts, big surface area. Palisade = lots of light, needs to photosynthesise → packed with chloroplasts, near the top. If you can picture the root underground and the leaf surface in sunlight, the features make sense.

keyPoints.heading: Differentiation — how a cell gets its job
keyPoints.content: Every cell in your body started from a single fertilised egg. So how does a nerve cell end up so different from a red blood cell? The answer is differentiation: the process by which a cell develops a specialised structure to carry out a specific function. Every cell has the same DNA, but different genes get switched on or off in different cells. In a red blood cell, the genes for making haemoglobin get switched on; the gene for making a nucleus gets switched off. This is how one genome produces 200+ different cell types. Most animal cells lose the ability to differentiate early in development. Plant cells keep it for much longer — that's why you can grow a whole new plant from a cutting.
writeBullets: Differentiation = a cell developing a specialised structure for a specific function
All cells have the same DNA — different genes are switched on/off in different cells
Most animal cells differentiate early and then lose that ability
Plant cells retain the ability to differentiate throughout their life

keyTerms.def: The process by which a cell changes to develop a specialised structure suited to a particular function
An undifferentiated cell that can divide and develop into different specialised cell types

keyPoints.memTip: Differentiation = genes switching on and off. The KEY word to include in an answer is 'specialised' — the cell develops a specialised structure. Try writing this definition from memory: 'Differentiation is the process by which a cell develops a specialised structure to carry out a specific function.'

tables.title: Animal cell vs plant cell — features compared

1.question: Which THREE structures are found in plant cells but NOT in animal cells?
1.correctFeedback: Exactly — cell wall (cellulose), chloroplasts, and permanent vacuole. Those three identify a plant cell every time.
1.wrongFeedback: The three plant-only structures are: cell wall (made of cellulose), chloroplasts (for photosynthesis), and permanent vacuole (for support). Animal cells share the nucleus, mitochondria, ribosomes and cell membrane.

3.question: A root hair cell has no chloroplasts. What is the correct reason for this?
options: Chloroplasts are too large to fit in root hair cells
Root hair cells are underground and receive no light, so photosynthesis cannot occur
Root hair cells already have a large surface area, so chloroplasts are not needed
Root hair cells photosynthesise using mitochondria instead
3.correctFeedback: Right — no light underground means no photosynthesis, so there's no need for chloroplasts.
3.wrongFeedback: The key is WHERE the cell is. Root hair cells are underground — no light reaches them, so photosynthesis can't happen. Having no chloroplasts is an adaptation to that environment, not a limitation on size.

commonMistakes: Saying plant cells always have chloroplasts — root hair cells and other non-photosynthetic plant cells don't. Only cells that photosynthesise need them.
Confusing cell wall with cell membrane — they are different structures. The cell membrane controls what enters and leaves; the cell wall provides rigid support. Both are present in plant cells.
Forgetting that red blood cells have no nucleus — this is deliberate, not a mistake. It creates more room for haemoglobin.
Writing 'nucleus' as one of the things a plant cell has that an animal cell doesn't — both have a nucleus. Only list cell wall, chloroplasts, and permanent vacuole.
Saying differentiation means cells change their DNA — they don't. The DNA is identical in every cell; what changes is which genes are switched on or off.
examTips: When asked why a cell has a particular feature, always link structure to function — e.g. 'the biconcave shape increases surface area, allowing more oxygen to be absorbed and released quickly.'
If asked to compare animal and plant cells, structure your answer: say what they share first, then the three plant-only extras. That order is cleaner and scores method marks.
Differentiation questions often ask you to explain it at the gene level — make sure to say 'genes being switched on and off' rather than just 'the cell changes'.

revisionCardBullets: Plant-only structures (CCV): Cell wall (cellulose), Chloroplasts (photosynthesis), permanent Vacuole (cell sap, turgidity)
Both cell types share: nucleus, cytoplasm, cell membrane, mitochondria, ribosomes
Sperm: acrosome (enzymes to enter egg), mitochondria (energy for swimming), tail (flagellum for movement)
Egg: large cytoplasm (nutrients), zona pellucida (hardens after fertilisation to block more sperm)
Red blood cell: biconcave disc (↑ surface area), no nucleus (more room for haemoglobin), packed with haemoglobin
Nerve cell: long axon (long-distance signal), myelin sheath (insulation, faster signal), synaptic knobs (pass signal on)
Root hair cell: long extension (↑ surface area for absorption), NO chloroplasts (no light underground)
Palisade mesophyll: packed with chloroplasts, at top of leaf to catch maximum light
Differentiation = cell develops specialised structure by switching genes on/off — same DNA throughout
Animal cells lose ability to differentiate early; plant cells retain it much longer
